skill.name: sql-expert
skill.description: Write, optimize, and debug T-SQL queries for Microsoft SQL Server using focused reference guidance for query patterns, performance, security, data types, and transactions.
skill.summary: Expert T-SQL guidance for SQL Server queries, tuning, security patterns, and transaction design.
skill.author: hmohamed01

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity patterns, but review found documentation-only Markdown with T-SQL examples. The alerts are false positives from SQL code fences, SQL Server keywords, and defensive security guidance; no host code execution, real network access, credential access, obfuscation, or prompt injection evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown SQL Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static detections for Ruby or shell backtick execution are false positives. The matched lines are Markdown code fences and T-SQL examples, not executable host-language commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched syntax is standard Markdown fencing around SQL snippets. No script file or host command execution path exists in these locations.

low_findings.title: SQL FETCH Keyword Misclassified as Network Access
low_findings.description: The network finding is a false positive caused by the SQL Server OFFSET-FETCH pagination syntax. It is not a Fetch API call and does not contact any external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a SQL pagination example using FETCH NEXT. There is no JavaScript, URL, request body, or external destination.

low_findings.title: Defensive SQL Security Examples Misclassified as Malicious
low_findings.description: Sensitive, weak-crypto, and command-and-control alerts are false positives in educational SQL Server security guidance. The file demonstrates SQL injection risks, mitigation patterns, permissions, encryption, and hardening checks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms appear in defensive documentation with mitigations such as parameters, least privilege, and disabling risky features. I found no data theft or malware control flow.

low_findings.title: SQL Server Metadata Queries Misclassified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: System reconnaissance alerts are false positives. The references show SQL Server DMV queries for performance tuning, transaction state, and blocking analysis, which match the skill purpose.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The queries read SQL Server system views for database tuning. They do not inspect the host machine or collect credentials.

low_findings.title: Distributed Transaction Examples Misclassified as Suspicious Remote Activity
low_findings.description: Remote and C2-related detections are false positives in transaction documentation. The examples discuss linked servers, distributed transactions, and compensation patterns in SQL Server systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The language is about database transaction coordination, not command-and-control infrastructure. No network client code, hidden endpoint, or exfiltration behavior is present.

content.value_statement: SQL Server work often needs fast, safe, and maintainable T-SQL under real production constraints. This skill gives structured guidance for query design, tuning, security, data types, and transactions.
actual_capabilities: Guides T-SQL query writing with CTEs, window functions, PIVOT, MERGE, and APPLY patterns.
Explains SQL Server execution plan symptoms, missing indexes, Query Store, and wait statistics.
Provides defensive SQL guidance for parameterized queries, dynamic SQL validation, and least privilege.
Covers SQL Server data type choices, conversions, collation handling, and storage tradeoffs.
Documents transaction patterns, isolation levels, deadlock prevention, and distributed transaction options.
limitations: Does not connect to databases or execute queries directly.
Requires schema, indexes, row counts, and SQL Server version for precise recommendations.
Focuses on Microsoft SQL Server and T-SQL, not general SQL across all database engines.
Cannot verify runtime performance without actual plans, statistics, or workload data.

use_cases.title: Build advanced T-SQL queries
use_cases.description: Create maintainable queries that use CTEs, window functions, pivots, and APPLY operators for complex reporting or data workflows.

use_cases.title: Write safer stored procedures
use_cases.description: Design procedures with parameterized dynamic SQL, clear error handling, transaction control, and safer permission boundaries.

use_cases.title: Diagnose performance problems
use_cases.description: Review slow query symptoms, execution plan operators, index choices, Query Store data, and wait statistics with SQL Server context.

prompt_templates.title: Write a basic query
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a T-SQL query for SQL Server. The tables are [tables], the relationships are [relationships], and the required result is [result].

prompt_templates.title: Improve query structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this T-SQL query for readability and maintainability. Suggest better joins, CTEs, window functions, or APPLY patterns where useful.

prompt_templates.title: Tune slow SQL Server work
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this slow SQL Server query. I can provide indexes, row counts, execution plan operators, logical reads, and SQL Server version.

prompt_templates.title: Design safe dynamic SQL
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a secure stored procedure that uses dynamic filters or sorting. Include parameterization, whitelist validation, error handling, and transaction guidance.

output_examples.input: A reporting query is slow because it filters dates by year and scans a large orders table.
output_examples.output: The response explains why the predicate is not index-friendly, proposes a date range filter, recommends a supporting index, and asks for the actual execution plan.

output_examples.input: A team needs optional filters and dynamic sorting in a SQL Server stored procedure.
output_examples.output: The response outlines a safer procedure design using parameters for values, a whitelist for sortable columns, quoted identifiers, and clear validation errors.

output_examples.input: A transaction sometimes deadlocks during order processing.
output_examples.output: The response identifies likely lock ordering issues, suggests shorter transaction scope, recommends consistent object access order, and describes a bounded retry strategy.

best_practices: Provide table definitions, indexes, SQL Server version, row counts, and sample requirements before asking for tuning help.
Ask for the reasoning behind each recommendation so tradeoffs are clear before applying changes.
Validate generated T-SQL in a non-production environment with representative data and actual execution plans.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for performance fixes without schema, indexes, row counts, or execution plan context.
Do not paste production secrets, credentials, or sensitive customer data into prompts.
Do not apply transaction, locking, or isolation changes without testing concurrency effects.

faq.question: Which database engine does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Microsoft SQL Server and T-SQL. Some concepts transfer, but syntax and tuning guidance are SQL Server specific.

faq.question: Can it execute SQL against my database?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples only. You must run and test any query in your own environment.

faq.question: What should I provide for query tuning?
faq.answer: Provide the query goal, table definitions, indexes, approximate row counts, SQL Server version, and actual plan details when available.

faq.question: Can it help prevent SQL injection?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for parameterized queries, safer dynamic SQL, identifier whitelists, and least privilege patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover stored procedures?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers procedure structure, error handling, transactions, dynamic SQL, permissions, and maintainability concerns.

faq.question: Can it help with deadlocks and isolation levels?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains isolation levels, lock hints, transaction scope, deadlock prevention, and retry handling for SQL Server workloads.
